CalifOrnia
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
October 23′  ■99■
Jean Leonard Harris
city Attorney
city of Rancho Mirage
69-a25 Highway 111
Rancho uirage, cA 9227 O
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our rile No. I -91-3 71
Dear Mr. Harris:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of a
city councilmember and redevelopment agency director regarding his
responsibilities under the confl. ict-of-interest 
provisions of the
Political Reforn Act (the nasgtt1.1 Since your letter fails to
identify the person on whose behalf you are requesting advice and
is in the nature of general assistance, we consider your letter to
be a request for infonnal^ assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329 (c) (copy enclosed) . z
OUESTIONS
A nember of a horaeowners' association, who also sits on the
association,s board of directors, is a city councilmenber and
redeveloprnent agency director.
1.. litay this person, in his capacity as a director of the
honeovrners, association, participate in decisions regarding how
the association is to proceed against the city's redevelopment
agency?
1 cora.nr.nt Code Sections 81OOO-91O15. Al1 statutory references
are to the covernnent Code unless otherwise indicated. Conmission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seo. AIl references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
inrnunity provided by an opinion or fornal written advice.
(Section 83113; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street,Suite 800 0 P。0。Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804Ю 807 ● (916)322・5660


Our File No. I-9■
-37■
Page 2
2. Hay this person, in his capacity as a director of the
city,s redevelopnent agency, participate in decisions regarding
hor.ir the city is to proceed against the houeowners, association in
connection with the associationrs request for annual dues and
other fees?
CONCLUSIONS
1. llembers of the board of directors of the homeovrners,
association are not tr Denbers of a governnent agencyr and are not
"public officials'r under the Act. Therefore, they ire not subject
to the conflict-of-i.nterest provisions of the Act.
2. Assuming a significant segment of the population of the
city are not nenbers of the homeowners, association, the
councilrnenber/director nust disqualify hinself f ron participating
in any redevelopnent agency decisions which concern the
houeowners, association,s request for annual dues and other fees.
FACTS
A counclhnenber/redevelopnent agency director sits on the
board of directors of a hoEeowrers, associatlon. He receives no
renuneration 1n this capacity. He also is a nernber of the
association. The honeowners, assoclation has jurisdiction over a
large area within a district of the city vhich includes property
zoned for residential and conroerciaL uses. The covenants,
conditions, and restrictions (the nCC&Rrstr) of the honeowners,
association prohibit commercial uses upon certain of the
residential lots. 
The honeowners, association also reguires
annual dues fron each parcel as well as a fee, based upon square
footage, for any architectural structure erected within the area
governed by the CC&R, s.
About four years ago, the cityrs redevelopnent agency
conderoned several of the residential lots within the jurisdiction
of the honeowners, association for a city haIl and a cornmercial
site. Recently, the board of directors of the honeowners,
association requested the pa)ment of annual dues per residential
Iot and a fee to review architectural design upon these lots frorn
the city redevelopnent agency. Furthenoore, though the city hall
and connercial uses are consistent with the city,s general plan
and zoning for the area, they are inconsistent with the
homeowners, association CC&R's.
An apparent conflict no!, exists between the city,s
redevelopment agency and the honeowners, association. The cityrs
redevelopnent agency has opined that the property it condemned is
not restricted by the honeowners, associ.ation CC&Rrs and that the
annual dues per lot and other fees hentioned within those
docurnents are invalid as against the property owned by the cityrs
redevelopnent agency. The homeorners, association has alleged an
opposite posltion.


Our Fl■e No. エー9■-37■
Page 3
As a menber of the board of directors of the homeowners'
association, the councilnenber/director will be required to vote
on how the houreowners' association is to proceed against the
redevelopment agency. The denands of the homeowners' association
against the city's redevelopment agency will exceed $50,000 for
the architectural review fee alone. The fee for the city's entire
redevelopment project area Day exceed S100,000. The funds vrould
be used directly for the benefit of the association members. The
board of directors of the homeowners' association have total
discretion as to ho!, to use the funds. For example, the funds
could be used to elininate the honeolrners t association dues, or to
provide a direct rebate in the fonn of cash, or to provide sorne
other financlal benefit to each association nenber. The benefit
per association rnernber could foreseeably exceed $250.
Furthennore, although your letter does not expressly state
what the councilnember/director will be required to do in his
capacity as redevelopnent director, inplicit in the facts is that
he will be asked to participate in redevelopnent agency decisions
concerning the hoDeowners ' association's claims.
ANALYSTS
The Act provides that no publlc official sha11 nake,
participate in naking, or use his or her official position to
influence any governnental declsion in which he or she has a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) A public official is
def ined in the Act as follor.rs:
trPublic officialtr Deans every nenber, officer,
employee or consultant of a state or 1ocal
government agency, but does not include judges and
court coramissioners in the judicial branch of
governnent....
Sectlon 82048.
The rnernbers of the board of directors of the homeovrners'
association are not employees, officers or consultants. They are
rrmembersrr of the association, but are they menbers of a loca1
governrnent agency? section 82041 defines trlocal government
agency[ as a county, city or dlstrict of any kind, including a
schooL distrlct, or any other local or regional pofitical
subdivision, or any departEent, division, bureau, office, board,
commission or other agency of the foregoing. It seens clear that
the nembers of the board of directors of the honeowners'
association are not rrnenbers of a governroent agencyrt and are not
"public officialsrr under the Act. Therefore, they are not subject
to the confl ict-of-interest provi.sions of the Act, and the
councilrnenber/director's disqualification is not required under
Section 87100.
on the other hand, city councllnenbers and redevelopnent
agency directors are 'rpublic officialsr under the Act and are


Our Fl■e No. I-9■-37■
Page 4
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. The
Act provides a four-part test to deternine $rhethe-r a public
offi;ia1 has a conflict of interest in a governrneintal decision.
First, is the official considering Daking,, participating in
rnaking, or using his official position to influence a oovernmental
decision? (section 87100.) second, is it foreseeable that the
decision wilt affect the official's econonic interests? (section
87103.) Third, is the effect of the decision on the official's
econouic interests material? (Id.) Fourth, is the effect of the
decision on the official's econouic interests distin<ruishable fron
its effect on the public qenerallv? (Id.) Each of these elernents
ruust be net before there is a conflict of i.nterest.
A. Makinq or Participatinq In A Governmental Decision
An official nakes a governnental decision when he or she
votes, connits his agency to a course of action, enters into a
contract, or appoints soneone. (Regulation 18700(b).)
Participating in the uaking of a governnental decision includes,
aDong other things, advising or naking reconnendations to the
deciiion Eaker. (Regulation 18700(c).) An offlcial attenpts to
use his official position to influence a decision if, for the
purpose of influencing the declslon, he contacts or aPpears
Lef6re, or othenrise attenpts to influence, any Denber, officer,
employee or consultant of the agency. (Regulation 18700.1(a).)
If- tha counci Imernber/director has a conflict of interest, he is
prohibited fron all these actions. For example, the
counci lnerober/director nould be prohibited fron determining the
city,s strategy and course of action regarding the honeor^rners'
association's clains as well as from appearing before his own
agencies on behalf of the homeowners' association.
B. Foreseeable Financial Effect on An Econonic fnterest
The second issue is the foreseeability that the decision will
affect the councilnenber/director's economj-c interests. The
parameters of a public official's econo:aic interest are set forth
in section 87103. For the Purposes of his situation,
An official has a financial interest in a
decislon wlthin the neaning of section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision wilI
have a naterial financial effect, distinguishable
fron its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a roenber of hls or her inmediate fanily
or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
officlaL has a direct or lndirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.
(b) Any real property in r.rhich the pubJ.ic
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or nore.


Our Fi■e No. エー9■-37■
Page 5
(c) Any source of incone, other than gifts
and other than loans by a conmercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the publ,ic without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
doflars ($250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or pronised to the public official
within 12 Eonths prior to the tine when the
decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, Partner, trustee,
enployee, or holds any position of managenent.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or nore in value
provided to, received by, or pronised to the public
otticiat vithin 12 nonths prlor to the tirne when
the decision is raade.
The effect of a decislon is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. To be reasonably
foreseeable, the effect of a declslon Eust be nore than a mere
possibllity, hosever certalnty ls not required. (Downev-Cares v.
bownev connunitv Developrnent con. (1987) 195 cal. App. 3d 983,
989-9t1; Witt v. Uorrot (L977) 70 CaI. App. 3d 817; In re Thor- -
(1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198, copy enclosed.) The Act seeks to prevent
nore than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even
the appearance of a possible confLlct of interest. (Witt v'
Ugfry, supra at 823. )
Thus when a natter comes before the counci lmember/director
and he suspects he nay have a conflict of interest, he should
exanine thl rnatter to deterroine r.rhether any decision by hirn would
have a reasonably foreseeabfe effect on any of the economic
interests listed in Section 87103. There is no question under
the facts presented here that any decision by the redevelopnent
agency on the homeowners' associatlon's request for annual dues
and other fees will have a financial effect on the councilman/
director. The issue for our conslderation is whether the
financial effect of the decision is material
c. Material Flnancial Effect
The conmission has adopted a series of regulations to
deternine whether a financial effect is roaterial. In order to
apply the regulations in a particular case, it is necessary to
look at the econonic interest affected and then examine both the
direct and indirect effects of the decision on those interests.
For exanple, the effect of a decision is naterial if the decision
will result in the personal expenses, incone, assets, or
liabilities of a publlc official or a nenber of his inrnediate
fanily increasing or decreasing by at least 9250. (Regulation


Our File No. 
エー91-37■
Page 6
La702.1(a) (4).) Thus if decisions concerning the homeolrners,
association,s request for annual fees and dues will result in a
$25o benefit to the councilmember/director., then he may have to
disqualify hinself from participating in those decisions.
D. rtPublic Generallv" Exception
Even if the decision before the counci lnember/director neets
alL the above elenents, his disqua l ification is required only if
the effect upon his econornic interests is distinguishable fron the
effect upon the public generally. (section 87103.) The rrpublicl
is all persons residing, owning property, or doing business in the
jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9
FPPC Ops. 1, 15.) In the case of a city counci 1/ redevelopnent
agency, this hrould be the entire city. 
If the decision does not
affect aII rnenbers of the public ln the same nanner, his
disqua I ification nay be required unless the effect of the decision
is the sane as the effect on a significant segment of the public.
(Regulatlon 18703, copy enclosed.) Therefore for the public
generally exception to apply, any decision would have to affect a
significant segrent of the city 1n substantlally the saDe manner
as it would affect the councilnenber/director.
Applylng this eleraent to the situatlon at hand, a significant
segment of the population of the city rnust be nenbers of the
honeowners' association in order to trigger the public generally
exception. You indicated in your letter that the district was
Iarge. That statenent is insufficient for us to ascertain if a
significant segnent of the population resides in the district 
arri.l
are members of the honeowners' association. Assumj.ng the district
does not represent a sigrnificant segnent of the population for the
public generally exception to apply, the counci lmernber/director
roust disqualify himself fron participating in any redevelopment
agency decisions lrhich concern the honeowners, association's
request for annual dues and other fees.
Questions about inconpatible offices should be directed to
your loca1 county counsel or the Attorney General
Sincerely yours,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral Counsel
ん
By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Lega1 Division
SH:DS:aa
